1.synthesis: Paul warns of "times of difficulty" in "the last days" (v.1), and gives a sobering catalogue: people will be "lovers of self, lovers of money, proud, arrogant, abusive… lovers of pleasure rather than lovers of God, having the appearance of godliness, but denying its power" (vv.2–5). "Avoid such people" (v.5) — those who creep into households and capture vulnerable people "always learning and never able to arrive at a knowledge of the truth" (vv.6–7). Like Jannes and Jambres opposing Moses, these "oppose the truth," but "they will not get very far, for their folly will be plain to all" (vv.8–9). The decline is real, but its end is certain exposure.
1.historical_context: The "last days" (v.1) and the moral-collapse list (vv.2–5) draw on the established "signs of the end" tradition in Jewish and early Christian eschatology, but Paul applies it to the present crisis, not a distant future — the last days began at Pentecost (Acts 2:17). "A form of godliness but denying its power" (v.5) names a counterfeit piety: religious vocabulary without the Spirit-wrought transformation the gospel produces. "Weak women" captured in households (v.6) reflects the false teachers' actual tactics in Ephesus. Jannes and Jambres (v.8), named from Jewish tradition as Pharaoh's magicians who opposed Moses (cf. Exodus 7:11), become the type of those who resist the truth.
1.literary_structure: The vice-list (vv.2–4) is framed by an opening "lovers of self" and a closing "lovers of pleasure rather than lovers of God," exposing disordered love as the root of the corruption. The command "avoid such people" (v.5) makes clear that the description is not abstract prophecy but present pastoral instruction about the false teachers in Timothy's own setting. The Jannes-and-Jambres comparison (vv.8–9) supplies a typological precedent and an assurance of limits, setting up the sharp contrast "But as for you" that opens the next section (v.10).
1.theological_application: The passage exposes the danger of a religion that has "the appearance of godliness" but denies "its power" (v.5): outward forms without inward transformation, a peril for every church and heart. The root of the predicted decline is misdirected love — loving self, money, and pleasure rather than God (vv.2–4) — a diagnosis as searching today as then. Yet the chapter steadies the faithful: error "will not get very far" (v.9), for like the magicians who withstood Moses, its folly is finally exposed. Discernment, including knowing when to "avoid" certain people (v.5), is part of faithfulness.
1.christology: The "power" that empty religion denies (v.5) is the transforming presence of the Spirit through the gospel of Christ (cf. Romans 1:16): to deny that power is to evacuate godliness of its life-giving Christological center. The "last days" are the age between Christ's two appearings (v.1), so the moral struggle Timothy faces is the normal texture of life in the interval before the Lord's return. And the certainty that opposition to the truth will be unmasked (v.9) rests on the triumph of the risen Christ, whose truth no counterfeit can finally overthrow.

10.pericope_label: Continue in What You Have Learned
10.synthesis: "You, however, have followed my teaching, my conduct, my aim in life, my faith, my patience, my love, my steadfastness, my persecutions and sufferings" (vv.10–11), from which the Lord rescued Paul — "and indeed all who desire to live a godly life in Christ Jesus will be persecuted" (v.12), while evil people and impostors go from bad to worse (v.13). Timothy must "continue in what you have learned… knowing from whom you learned it," having known "the sacred writings" from childhood, "which are able to make you wise for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus" (vv.14–15). For "all Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete, equipped for every good work" (vv.16–17).
10.historical_context: "You have followed" (parēkolouthēsas, v.10) means to trace closely as an apprentice tracks a master; the nine-item list presents Paul's life, not only his doctrine, as the curriculum. The universal claim that the godly "will be persecuted" (v.12) echoes Jesus' own teaching (John 15:20). "From childhood you have known the sacred writings" (hiera grammata, v.15) reflects Timothy's Jewish-family formation in the Hebrew Scriptures (cf. 2 Timothy 1:5). "Breathed out by God" (theopneustos, v.16) is a Pauline coinage meaning Scripture is the product of the divine breath, recalling God breathing life into Adam (Genesis 2:7). "Complete" and "equipped" (v.17) describe one thoroughly fitted for action.
10.literary_structure: "But as for you" (v.10) sets Timothy in sharp contrast to the impostors of vv.1–9, grounding his stability in two sources: the apostolic example he has followed (vv.10–11) and the Scriptures he has known from infancy (v.15). The command "continue" (mene, v.14) — the same verb as John's "abide" — answers the false teachers' drift. The section climaxes in the classic statement on Scripture's inspiration and sufficiency (vv.16–17), which grounds the entire letter's insistence that Timothy guard and preach the word, and leads directly into the solemn charge of 2 Timothy 4:1–2.
10.theological_application: Persecution is the normal lot of those who "desire to live a godly life in Christ Jesus" (v.12) — a sobering corrective to any expectation that faithfulness will bring the world's approval. Against drift, the believer is to "continue" in what was learned (v.14), anchored both in trusted teachers and, supremely, in Scripture. The great affirmation of vv.16–17 grounds the Christian's confidence in the Bible: because all Scripture is God-breathed, it is profitable to teach, reprove, correct, and train, fully equipping the believer "for every good work" — doctrine and life together.
10.christology: The Scriptures' saving purpose is explicitly Christ-centered: they "are able to make you wise for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus" (v.15) — the Old Testament Timothy knew from infancy is the Christological preparation for the gospel, its wisdom finding its goal in him (cf. Romans 10:4). Paul's example, which Timothy has followed (v.10), is itself a transparency through which Christ's character — faithful, patient, loving, enduring — is seen, so that to imitate Paul is to imitate the Lord he imitates (1 Corinthians 11:1). The God-breathed Scripture is the instrument that serves the proclamation of the Christ it anticipates.
